coredata.dc:title: Ultraweak chemiluminescence enhanced on the surface of lanthanide metal–organic framework nanosheets synthesized by ultrasonic wave
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Chunxia
Wang, Yanran
Wang, Yufei
Chen, Tianyou
Wu, Jing
Liu, Xuanhe
Lin, Caihou
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, lanthanide metal–organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs, Ln = Ce, Gd, Ho) were facilely synthesized by ultrasonic method and applied in enhancing chemiluminescence (CL) emission of NaIO4-H2O2 system. The synthesized Ln-MOFs were characterized to be nanosheet-like shapes. Comparing with the NaIO4-H2O2 system without Ln-MOFs, the CL intensities of Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems were enhanced 28, 32 and 67 times by Ce-MOFs, Gd-MOFs and Ho-MOFs, respectively. Catalytic properties of Ln-MOFs accounted for the improved performance in CL and increased the generation of some reactive oxygen species (ROS) (·O2 −, 1O2 and ·OH) which induced CL enhancement. The large surface area of nanosheets and structural defects generated during the ultrasonic synthesis process could facilitate the exposure of catalytic active sites and ROS generation. This work gave insights into developing novel CL systems based on advanced materials with effective surfaces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MOFs
Ultrasonic synthesis
Chemiluminescence
Nanosheet
Large surface
Reactive oxygen species

CL spectra of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems
CL mechanism study of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems
CL emission of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems
CL mechanisms of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We greatly acknowledge for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874120); the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2652019112 and 2652018004); the open fund of Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (No. OESACLS202004); and Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (No. 2018Y9024).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of catalysis of ROS generation on surfaces of ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs and their application in enhancing CL of NaIO4-H2O2 system.
Characterization of ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs. (a-c) TEM images of Ln-MOFs; (d-f) EDS analysis of Ln-MOFs; (g) The detected and simulated XRD spectra of Ln-MOFs; (h) FT-IR spectra of Ln-MOFs.

CL emissions of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems. (a) The comparison of CL intensities of NaIO4-H2O2 system, LnCl3-NaIO4-H2O2 systems and Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems; (b) CL spectra of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems. Conditions: 0.1 M NaIO4, 2 mg LnCl3, 2 mg ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs and 0.3 M H2O2 in 0.01 M H2SO4.

Optimization of experimental conditions of Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems. (a-c) Additional sequence of reagents was optimized (1: 0.1 M NaIO4 was added into the mixture of 2 mg ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs and 0.3 M H2O2 (acidified by 0.01 M H2SO4); 2: 0.3 M H2O2 (acidified by 0.01 M H2SO4) was mixed with 2 mg ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs and 0.1 M NaIO4); (d) Concentration of H2SO4 was optimized (Conditions: 0.1 M NaIO4, 2 mg ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs and 0.3 M H2O2); (e) Concentration of H2O2 was optimized (Conditions: 0.1 M NaIO4, 2 mg ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs and 0.01 M H2SO4); (f) Concentration of NaIO4 was optimized (Conditions: 2 mg ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs and 0.3 M H2O2 in 0.01 M H2SO4). The standard error bars were calculated through three individual experiments.

CL intensity comparison of Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems using hydrothermal synthesized and ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs. (a) CL intensity comparison of Ce-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems using hydrothermal synthesized and ultrasonic synthesized Ce-MOFs. (b) CL intensity comparison of Gd-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems using hydrothermal synthesized and ultrasonic synthesized Gd-MOFs. (c) CL intensity comparison of Ho-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems using hydrothermal synthesized and ultrasonic synthesized Ho-MOFs. Conditions: 0.1 M NaIO4, 2 mg hydrothermal synthesized/ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs and 0.3 M H2O2 in 0.01 M H2SO4.

BET surface areas of Ln-MOFs were measured by detecting N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm at 77 K. (a-c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of hydrothermal synthesized Ln-MOFs; (d-f) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs.

Detection of free radicals in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems. (a) ·O2 − was detected by EPR spectra in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems with DMPO as the specific scavenger agent and CH3OH as the solvent. (b) 1O2 was detected by EPR spectra in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems with TEMP as the specific scavenger agent. (c) ·OH was detected by EPR spectra in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems with DMPO as the specific scavenger agent. (d) ·O2 − was detected by CL spectra in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems with BQ (5 mg) as the specific scavenger agent. (e) ·OH was detected by CL spectra in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems with Iso as the specific scavenger agent. (f) ·OH was detected by CL spectra in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems with Thi as the specific scavenger agent. Conditions: 0.1 M NaIO4, 2 mg ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs and 0.3 M H2O2 in 0.01 M H2SO4.
Schematic of the CL mechanism in the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems.

head.ce:title: Ultraweak chemiluminescence enhanced on the surface of lanthanide metal–organic framework nanosheets synthesized by ultrasonic wave

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China University of Geosciences (Beijing), Beijing 100083, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, China University of Geosciences (Beijing), Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Neurosurgery, Fujian Medical University Union Hospital, Fuzhou, Fujian 350001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Neurosurgery, Fujian Medical University Union Hospital, Fuzhou, Fujian 350001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lanthanide metal–organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) were facilely synthesized by ultrasonic wave and exhibited large surfaces which were aiding for enhancing chemiluminescence (CL) of NaIO4-H2O2 system.

In this paper, lanthanide metal–organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs, Ln = Ce, Gd, Ho) were facilely synthesized by ultrasonic method and applied in enhancing chemiluminescence (CL) emission of NaIO4-H2O2 system. The synthesized Ln-MOFs were characterized to be nanosheet-like shapes. Comparing with the NaIO4-H2O2 system without Ln-MOFs, the CL intensities of Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems were enhanced 28, 32 and 67 times by Ce-MOFs, Gd-MOFs and Ho-MOFs, respectively. Catalytic properties of Ln-MOFs accounted for the improved performance in CL and increased the generation of some reactive oxygen species (ROS) (·O2 −, 1O2 and ·OH) which induced CL enhancement. The large surface area of nanosheets and structural defects generated during the ultrasonic synthesis process could facilitate the exposure of catalytic active sites and ROS generation. This work gave insights into developing novel CL systems based on advanced materials with effective surfaces.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) is a luminescent process generated during chemical reactions and developed to be an analytical method with advantages of high sensitivity, fast speed, good responsibility, convenient operation, low instrument cost and low background signal [1]. At present, CL has been widely used as an analytical method in the fields of environmental science, life science, food detection, clinical diagnosis, and so on [2–6]. Sensitivity is the key of CL analysis while CL intensity is weak in general. Therefore, researchers give persistent efforts in exploring various strategies to obtain strong CL signals to improve sensitivity of CL analysis [7–10]. Utilization of advanced materials to enhance CL emission is proved to be an ingenious approach and sheds light on designing sensitive CL analytical methods. Among the multiple advanced materials, nanomaterials are the commonly used ones due to their unique morphologies and remarkable chemical and physical properties [11]. Cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr3) perovskite quantum dots (QDs) were synthesized and employed in developing a hypersensitive photosensor to realize CL immunoassays for the detection of human hepatitis B surface antigen, human immunodeficiency virus antibody and alpha-fetoprotein [12]. CuInS2@ZnS nanocrystals were employed as emitters in a near-infrared aqueous CL strategy to construct a signal-off CL biosensor for ascorbic acid analysis [13]. CL signals also were utilized to monitor environmental toxins. Europium-doping CeO2 nanoparticles (NPs) were designed for SO3 2− detection in PM2.5 based on CL resonance energy transfer (CRET) process [14]. Core/shell Fe3O4-decorated Pd NPs hybrids were prepared and enwrapped by hollow Fe-metal organic frameworks (MOFs) to obtain a composite (Pd@Fe3O4@MOFs) with yolk/shell structure. Pd@Fe3O4@MOFs degraded chlorophenols and phenols by generating ·OH which was detected by a continuous-flow CL system [15]. The toxic intermediates generated in the photocatalytic degradation of pentachlorophenol (PCP) also were monitored by a continuous-flow CL system by pumping PCP/TiO2 suspension and mixing with H2O2 [16].

As one kind of crystalline nanoporous materials, the molecular structure and energy level of MOFs perfectly match with CL process [17]. MOFs are constructed through self-assembling metal ions with organic linkers. Post-synthesis modification can be realized by integrating functional molecules as the linkers and endows MOFs with many unique properties including high porosity, large surface area and many active sites [18,19]. These properties allow MOFs to be applied in sensors [20–22], catalytic reactions [23,24], drug delivery [25], gas storage and separation [26]. However, MOFs application in CL process is still in the exploratory stage due to the instability of MOFs in aqueous solutions. Besides that, hydro- and solvo-thermal reactions are the common methods used to synthesize MOFs and involve long reaction time and high temperature [27]. These energy- and time-consuming processes increase the difficulty of MOFs application in CL. Thus, it is urgent to explore economical and facile approaches for MOFs synthesis aiding for their application in CL. As a rapid and environment-friendly route, ultrasonic synthesis has been tried to synthesize MOFs which are applied as oxygen reduction reaction catalysts [24,28], energy harvesting nanomachines [29], gas sensor [30], optical sensor [31,32], electrochemiluminescence immunosensors [33] and so on. Nevertheless, until now, ultrasonic synthesized-MOFs were scarcely put into use in CL emission.

All the materials and apparatus used in this work were listed in detail in section 1 in supplementary information. Ln-MOFs were synthesized according to the previous literature utilizing ultrasonic wave [34]. Firstly, 32 mL N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was mixed with 2 mL H2O and 2 mL ethanol in a 100 mL glass bottle (Duran Schott). Subsequently, 0.75 mmol lanthanide salt (CeCl3·7H2O, GdCl3·6H2O, HoCl3·6H2O) and equivalent BDC were added into the bottle. 0.8 mL triethylamine (TEA) was infused into the solution after 5 min. Then, the glass bottle was keeping ultrasonic treatment for 8 h with the power of 80 kHz under ambient conditions. The resulting product was collected and centrifuged. After removing the supernatant, the resultant was washed with ethanol for 4 times and dried by vacuum freeze-drying. Ultrasonic power and time were optimized to obtain products with the optimum CL enhancement. Hydrothermal methods described in ref. [35] also were used to synthesize Ln-MOFs which were used as control.

In order to detect CL emission of Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems, 2 mg Ln-MOFs were dissolved in 1 mL 0.1 M NaIO4 solution. 1 mL H2O2 with concentration of 0.3 M (dissolved in 0.01 M sulfuric acid) was added into the above solution. In the control experiment, no Ln-MOFs was added aiming at detecting whether Ln-MOFs enhanced the CL signals or not. Control experiments also were conducted by adding lanthanide salts with the same quantity. The concentration of each substance in the CL system was optimized to obtain the best CL emission. Series of concentrations of H2SO4 (0, 0.005, 0.010, 0.015, 0.02 and 0.03 M), H2O2 (0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.50 and 1.00 M) and NaIO4 (0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.09, 0.10, 0.11 and 0.15 M) were tested to get the optimal conditions to carry out the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reagent injection sequence on CL
ce:section.ce:para: Various mixing orders of reagents were tested to evaluate the effect of reagent injection sequence on CL signals of Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems. CL signals were detected with two different injection sequences. One way is conducted by adding acidified H2O2 into the mixed solution of Ln-MOFs and NaIO4 and another way is performed by injecting NaIO4 into the mixture of Ln-MOFs and acidified H2O2. All the reagents were used with the optimal concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL spectra of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems
ce:section.ce:para: High-energy cutoff filters (400–640 nm) were utilized and set between the photomultiplier tube (PMT) and quartz cuvette to detect CL spectra. All the reagents were used with the optimal concentrations and injected with the optimal sequence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL mechanism study of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of the enhanced CL in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems was inferred by using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and free radical quenching experiments. EPR spectra were collected at an X-band frequency of 9.85 GHz. An optical fiber with length of 50 cm and diameter of 0.3 cm was focused on samples in the cavity. Xe lamp (300 W, 300 nm < λ < 1100 nm) was used as the light source. 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) (0.05 M in 0.05 M deoxygenized phosphate buffer) and 12.4 μL 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolineN-oxide (DMPO) (dissolved in 1 mL H2O) were used as the detection reagent specific for 1O2 and ∙OH, respectively. In the free radical quenching experiment, various radical scavengers with different concentrations were taken to explore the intermediates generated in the CL systems.

The morphologies of Ln-MOFs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2 a, 2b and 2c) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. S1a, S1b and S1c). Ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs displayed nanosheet-like shape which was different from the products obtained by hydrothermal method [35]. Nanosheet-like morphology exhibited large surface and numerous active sites which were beneficial for CL enhancement. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was conducted to further verify the elemental compositions of C, O and Ln in Ln-MOFs (Fig. 2d, e and f). The characteristic diffraction peaks (9.0°, 14.8°, 15.4° and 18.4°) of BDC-based MOFs displayed in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the three different kinds of Ln-MOFs (Fig. 2g) demonstrating their highly crystalline structures which also were proved by the perfect diffraction dots of electrons in selected-area electron diffraction (SEAD) images (Fig. S1d, S1e and S1f) [36]. The XRD patterns of the three different kinds of Ln-MOFs displayed almost the same diffraction peaks (Fig. 2g) which matched well with the simulated ones of Ln2(BDC)3∙(H2O)4 (Ln = Ce, Gd, Ho) MOFs [37]. Because of the high coordination number of Ln3+ ions and the multidentate functionality of BDC, Ln3+ ions coordinated to BDC ligands together with the water molecules constructing the eight-coordination. In these Ln-MOFs, BDC acted as not only the ligands with Ln but also the linkers extending the clusters of the MOFs [35]. The formation of Ln-MOFs was confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in Fig. 2h. The peaks at 1669 cm−1 and 1539 cm−1 were assigned to the vibrations of C = O which showed a red shift than that of BDC demonstrating the successful coordination between the central Ln ions and ligands. The narrow intense peaks locating at 1410 cm−1 was ascribed to the vibration of − C − O − C − O which disappeared in the ligand of BDC proving the successful formation of Ln-MOFs again [35]. The sharp peaks at 750 cm−1 suggested the bending vibration of C − H bonds in benzene ring of BDC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL emission of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems
ce:section.ce:para: At first, the CL enhancement of Ln-MOFs in NaIO4-H2O2 system was verified. The CL signal of NaIO4-H2O2 systems was very weak while it was greatly enhanced by ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs (Fig. 3 a). CL signals enhanced by free Ln3+ (Ln = Ce, Gd, Ho) ions also were detected. LnCl3 (Ln = Ce, Gd, Ho) salt solutions were added into the NaIO4-H2O2 system, respectively. It was obvious that in Fig. 3a, the sensitization of free Ln3+ was negligible indicating the enhancement effect was derived from Ln-MOFs. The CL emission spectra of the three systems centered at 550 nm which had no overlap with photoluminescence (PL) emissions of Ln-MOFs (Fig. 3b and S2). These data indicated that Ln-MOFs were not the main light emission species and the CL emission did not happen via CRET approach. The CL intensities were investigated with Ln-MOFs synthesized with different ultrasonic power and time (Fig. S3). Ln-MOFs treated by ultrasonic wave with the power of 80 kHz and time of 8 h exhibited the best CL enhancement. CL enhancement of ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs also was tested in NaClO-H2O2, luminol-H2O2 and NaNO2-H2O2 systems while the CL intensities of these systems were hardly enhanced by Ln-MOFs (Fig. S4). Ln-MOFs exhibited high selectivity to enhance the CL signals of NaIO4-H2O2 system.

In order to obtain the optimum CL signals, all the experimental conditions were optimized. At first, the additional sequence of reagents was investigated (Fig. 4 a-4c). The CL signals were weak when NaIO4 was injected into the mixture of Ln-MOFs and acidified H2O2. The remarkably enhanced CL was monitored with the injection of acidified H2O2 into the mixture of Ln-MOFs and NaIO4. It was referred that H2O2 decomposition was catalyzed by Ln-MOFs and generated some active intermediates which immediately reacted with the active species derived from NaIO4 to give enhanced CL emission using the second injection order. While in the first injection order, the active intermediates released and did not join the CL reactions in time because of the latter injection of NaIO4. The effect of H2SO4 on the CL signals of Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems were detected and shown in Fig. 4d. In the absence of H2SO4, no obvious CL was observed. CL intensities were enhanced with the increased concentration of H2SO4 until the concentration reached 0.01 M demonstrating acidified environment was beneficial for CL emission. The CL signals also were amplified by H2O2 with the concentration ranging from 0.005 M to 0.3 M (Fig. 4e). The optimal signal-to-noise ratio was obtained with NaIO4 at a concentration of 0.1 M (Fig. 4f). Hence, the optimal conditions for the CL systems of Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 were 0.3 M H2O2 (acidified by 0.01 M H2SO4) added into the mixture of 2 mg Ln-MOFs and 0.1 M NaIO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL mechanisms of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems
ce:section.ce:para: In CL spectra, the maximum emission located at the position of ∼ 550 nm proving that the luminophor molecules in the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems were dimeric singlet oxygen (1O2)2* [38–39] which were derived from three common ROS (·O2 −, 1O2 and ·OH). ROS are commonly involved in the redox reactions in CL systems containing H2O2 [40]. Room-temperature EPR spectroscopy and the effects of different radical scavengers on the CL intensity of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems were investigated to detect the three kinds of ROS in these CL systems. DMPO which was the specific detection reagent for ·O2 − and ·OH was utilized to examine the variations of the two radicals with the addition of Ln-MOFs depending on EPR spectroscopy. In this work, the presence of ·O2 − in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems was confirmed by the presence of characteristic 1:1:1:1 quartette EPR signals of DMPO−·O2 − (Fig. 7 a). Besides that, BQ which were common scavenger of ·O2 − caused more than 90% inhibition of intensity indicating the important role of ·O2 − in the CL emission process (Fig. 7d). The typical 1:1:1 triplet signal of TEMP-1O2 in Fig. 7b proved the existence of 1O2 in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems. The EPR signal of TEMP-1O2 in Ho-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 system was stronger than that in other two CL systems. 1O2 was the source for generating luminophor molecule (1O2)2*. The strongest EPR signal of 1O2 in Ho-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 system explained why CL emission of Ho-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 system was the highest. The presence of ·OH in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems was proved by the characteristic 1:2:2:1 EPR signals of DMPO-·OH (marked with asterisk symbols) which were absence in NaIO4-H2O2 systems (Fig. 7c) suggesting the decomposition of H2O2 was facilitated by Ln-MOFs and then generated ·OH. The triplet signals in Fig. 7c derived from 5,5-dimethyl-2-oxopyrroline-1-oxyl (DMPOX) which was the oxidation product of reaction between 1O2 and DMPO [41]. EPR signals of DMPOX in Ho-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 system was the strongest and agreed with the EPR signals of 1O2 in Fig. 7b demonstrating the most quantity of 1O2 generating in Ho-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 system due to the excellent catalytic property of Holmium [42,43]. Both Iso and Thi are scavengers of ·OH. The CL intensities were significantly decreased with the addition of Iso and Thi confirming the generation and effect of ·OH in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems (Fig. 7e and f). Comparing with NaIO4-H2O2 system, the intensities of EPR signals of the three radicals in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems were enhanced demonstrating Ln-MOFs worked like peroxidase which facilitated the generation of ROS. Ln-MOFs synthesized in this work exhibited high peroxidase-mimetic activities towards NaIO4-H2O2 system because of the high specific surface area of their nanosheet-like morphology. Catalytic moieties were immobilized in the pore surfaces and the inside space of MOFs. The catalytic efficiency of MOFs can be improved by the uniform dispersion of active metal sites on pore surfaces and in space [44]. The gradual generation of radicals and electron-transfer processes were accelerated by the catalytic properties of Ln-MOFs which catalyzed the reaction of NaIO4-H2O2 system to produce three kinds of active radicals (·O2 −, 1O2 and ·OH) via breaking the O-O bond of H2O2. Then, the stabilization and recombination of ·OH and ·O2 − could be realized on the surfaces or in the pores of Ln-MOFs to produce 1O2. As one kind of classical Cu-MOFs, HKUST-1 was reported to accelerate the radical generation and electron-transfer processes on their surfaces in a H2O2-containing CL system [45]. In this research, Ln-MOFs, we synthesized, exhibited the similar catalytic properties on their surfaces. No obvious light absorption was observed in UV–vis absorption spectra of the Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems (Fig. S6) indicating that all the generated species displayed no UV–vis absorption.

Based on the above study, the possible CL mechanism of Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems was summarized in equation (Eqs. (1)–(6)) and Fig. 8 . Firstly, IO4 − reacted with H2O2 to produce ·O2 − and IO3 − (Eq. (1)) [39,46,47]. Ln-MOFs specifically catalyzed NaIO4-H2O2 system (Fig. S4) possibly due to the first reaction was selectively catalyzed by Ln-MOFs and generated ·O2 − which was necessary for the subsequent reactions. ·O2 − reacted with H2O2 to generate ·OH (Eq. (2)) [47]. The recombination of ·O2 − and ·OH generated 1O2 (Eqs. (3) and (4)) [48,49]. The intrinsic catalytic property of Ln-MOFs accelerated the breakage of the O-O bond in H2O2 to produce ·OH (Eq. (5)) [40,50]. 1O2 was active and easily transformed to be excited (1O2)2* (Eq. (6)) [39]. The wide CL emission was derived from the excited oxygen dimol specie (1O2)2*.

→ h v

In summary, series of Ln-MOFs were facilely synthesized by ultrasonic wave and exhibited a nanosheet-like morphology. It demonstrated that ultraweak CL of the NaIO4-H2O2 system was enhanced by these ultrasonic synthesized Ln-MOFs. All the experimental conditions were optimized to obtain the most intense CL. Owing to the catalytic property, Ln-MOFs accelerated the decomposition of H2O2 and catalyzed some active radicals (·O2 −, 1O2 and ·OH) generation on the large surfaces of nanosheets. The enhanced CL in Ln-MOFs-NaIO4-H2O2 systems was attributed to the increased ROS generation. This work enriched the research on MOF catalysis and indicated the extension applications of this kind of materials with improved surface chemical engineering.

Chunxia Huang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yanran Wang: Formal analysis. Yufei Wang: Formal analysis. Tianyou Chen: Formal analysis. Jing Wu: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xuanhe Liu and Caihou Lin: Writing – review & editing. All authors approved the submission.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly acknowledge for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874120); the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2652019112 and 2652018004); the open fund of Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (No. OESACLS202004); and Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (No. 2018Y9024).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.151860.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
